# houstonbankruptcy.org -- Full Content

This file contains the full text content of all pages on houstonbankruptcy.org.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).


---

## Houston Bankruptcy -- Chapter 7 & 13 Filing Guide [2026]
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Houston Bankruptcy -- Your Complete Filing Guide for 2026

You are not alone. Over 91,806 Houston area residents have filed for bankruptcy since 2008. This free guide will help you understand your options, know what to expect, and make informed decisions.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

91,806

S.D. Tex. Cases (2008-2024)

65.1%

Dismissal Rate

34.9%

Discharge Rate

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 -- Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on your income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Learn more about Chapter 7 →

Chapter 13 -- Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure or a car from repossession. No means test required.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

No means test requirement

Filing fee: $313

Learn more about Chapter 13 →

Not sure which chapter fits? Compare them in detail.

S.D. Tex. Bankruptcy Data

The numbers tell an important story. The Southern District of Texas has processed 91,806 Chapter 13 cases since 2008.

Southern District of Texas (S.D. Tex.)

65.1% dismissed

91,806 cases filed since 2008. Only 34.9% achieved a successful discharge. The prior filer rate is 30.6% -- meaning roughly 1 in 4 filers have been through this before.

Courthouse: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

Key Indicators

30.6% prior filers

An estimated 2,622 cases may involve potential 1328(f) eligibility issues -- filings where the debtor had a prior discharge within the statutory time bar. The pro se rate is 10.8%.

Data: FJC Integrated Database, 2008-2024

Full statistics and analysis →

What to Expect When Filing

The Process at a Glance

Credit counseling -- You must complete a credit counseling course within 180 days before filing. Cost: about $15-25.

File your petition -- Your attorney files the petition, schedules, and statements with the court. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, you attend a brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education course -- You must complete a second financial education course before discharge. Cost: about $10-25.

Discharge -- Chapter 7: about 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Detailed timeline and what to expect →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost?

The total cost depends on the chapter you file and your specific situation:

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (can request installment payments)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,500 for Chapter 7; $3,000-$5,000 for Chapter 13 in Houston

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Full cost breakdown → | National cost guide

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Houston?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in Houston typically range from $1,000-$2,500 for Chapter 7 and $3,000-$5,000 for Chapter 13. Credit counseling costs about $25-50 total. Full cost breakdown.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Texas?

Texas's homestead exemption protects Unlimited (10 acres urban). In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed payments through your plan. See all exemptions.

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy?

Texas allows a vehicle exemption of $0 -- fully exempt if needed for work. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments through your plan. Learn about exemptions.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. The 341 meeting of creditors usually happens about 30 days after filing. Full timeline.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt quickly but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 allows you to keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Your income, assets, and type of debt determine which chapter is best. Compare chapters.

What is the dismissal rate in the S.D. Tex.?

Based on data from 91,806 cases, the S.D. Tex. has a 65.1% dismissal rate. This means many filers never complete their plan. Full statistics.

See all frequently asked questions →

Need Free Bankruptcy Help?

If you cannot afford a bankruptcy attorney, free help is available. Legal aid organizations, law school clinics, and court self-help programs can assist you at no cost.

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Houston -- Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/chapter-7.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Houston

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what Houston residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors -- but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

In Houston, the vast majority of Chapter 7 filers keep all their property because it falls within Texas exemption limits.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Old utility bills

Deficiency balances from repossessions

Some older tax debts

Civil judgments (in most cases)

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court-ordered restitution and fines

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Certain debts under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)

The Means Test

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the state median:

Below median income: You automatically qualify for Chapter 7

Above median income: A detailed calculation of your disposable income determines eligibility. If you have enough disposable income to fund a Chapter 13 plan, you may be required to file Chapter 13 instead

For a single filer in Texas, the current median is approximately $56,000/year. These figures increase with household size.

Texas Exemptions in Chapter 7

Exemptions determine what property you keep. Texas requires you to use state exemptions -- you cannot choose the federal exemption system.

PropertyTexas ExemptionStatute

HomesteadUnlimited (10 acres urban / 100 acres rural)Tex. Prop. Code 41.001

VehicleFully exempt (1 per licensed household member)Tex. Prop. Code 42.002

Personal property (aggregate)$60,000 married / $30,000 singleTex. Prop. Code 42.001

ClothingIncluded in personal propertyTex. Prop. Code 42.002

Tools of tradeUnlimitedTex. Prop. Code 42.002

Retirement accounts (IRA, 401k, pension)UnlimitedTex. Prop. Code 42.0021

Social Security benefitsUnlimited42 U.S.C. 407

Wages (current)Unlimited (non-garnishable in Texas)Tex. Prop. Code 42.001

Life insurance (cash value)UnlimitedTex. Ins. Code 1108.051

Health aidsUnlimitedTex. Prop. Code 42.002

Full exemption details.

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-2: Preparation -- Gather documents (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements). Complete credit counseling course ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing -- Petition filed with the court. Automatic stay takes effect immediately -- creditors must stop all collection activity, garnishments, lawsuits, and foreclosure proceedings.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting -- Brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath about your finances. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Day 30-60: Debtor education -- Complete the second required financial education course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge -- The court enters your discharge order, eliminating eligible debts. Case closed shortly after.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans (in most cases), recent taxes, child support, alimony, or debts incurred through fraud. See section 523(a) for the full list.

What is the means test for Chapter 7?

The means test compares your household income to the Texas median. If your income is below the median for your household size, you automatically qualify. If above, a more detailed calculation determines whether you have enough disposable income to fund a Chapter 13 plan instead.

How long does Chapter 7 take?

From filing to discharge, Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. The 341 meeting of creditors occurs about 30 days after filing, and the discharge is usually entered about 60 days after that.

What property can I keep in Chapter 7 in Texas?

Texas exemptions protect: homestead equity of Unlimited (10 acres urban), vehicle equity of $0 -- fully exempt if needed for work, personal property of $60,000 (married) / $30,000 (single), and retirement accounts. Full exemption details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Houston -- Payment Plans, Dismissal Rates, Timeline
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/chapter-13.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Houston

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. But in the S.D. Tex., the dismissal rate is 65.1% -- meaning many filers never complete their plan.

65.1% of S.D. Tex. Chapter 13 cases are dismissed

65.1%

S.D. Tex. Dismissal Rate

34.9%

Discharge Rate

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13, sometimes called a "wage earner's plan," allows people with regular income to create a court-supervised repayment plan lasting 3 to 5 years. You make monthly payments to a bankruptcy trustee, who distributes the money to your creditors according to the plan.

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not require liquidating assets. You keep all your property. At the end of the plan, remaining eligible debts are discharged.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful for:

Saving a home from foreclosure by catching up on missed mortgage payments

Keeping a car while catching up on auto loan payments

Paying tax debts or other priority debts over time

Protecting non-exempt property that would be lost in Chapter 7

Filers who do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test

S.D. Tex. Dismissal Rate

The data tells a concerning story. A dismissed Chapter 13 means the debtor spent months or years making payments but received no discharge -- no debt relief.

Southern District of Texas (S.D. Tex.)

65.1% dismissed

Out of 91,806 Chapter 13 cases filed between 2008 and 2024, only 34.9% achieved a successful discharge. The prior filer rate is 30.6% -- nearly 1 in 4 filers had a prior case.

Why it matters: A dismissed Chapter 13 means the automatic stay ends, creditors resume collection, and the debtor receives no discharge. Months or years of payments produced no lasting relief. Strong legal representation is critical to completing a Chapter 13 plan. View full statistics.

How a Chapter 13 Plan Works

The Monthly Payment

Your monthly payment is calculated based on your disposable income -- the difference between your income and your reasonable living expenses. The payment must cover:

Priority debts in full: Recent taxes, child support arrears, attorney fees

Secured debt arrears: Mortgage arrears, car loan arrears

Trustee fees: Typically around 10% of plan payments

Unsecured creditors: Whatever remains goes to credit cards, medical bills, etc. -- often pennies on the dollar

Plan Duration: 3 or 5 Years?

If your income is below the state median, you may be able to complete a 3-year plan. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The court will not approve a plan longer than 5 years.

What Happens at the End?

After completing all plan payments, the court enters a discharge under 11 U.S.C. section 1328. This eliminates remaining eligible unsecured debts. You keep your home, car, and other property.

If you received a prior discharge, time bars under section 1328(f) may prevent you from receiving another discharge. Check your eligibility before filing.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Complete credit counseling ($15-25). Gather income documentation, tax returns, and debt records.

Day 1 -- Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments stop immediately.

Day 14: Proposed plan filed (if not filed with the petition).

Day 30: First plan payment due to the trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting of creditors. The trustee reviews your plan and finances.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing. The court decides whether to approve your plan.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments to the trustee. Report income changes. File tax returns on time.

Plan completion: Complete debtor education course. Court enters discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Chapter 13 dismissal rate in Houston?

The Southern District of Texas has a 65.1% Chapter 13 dismissal rate based on 2008-2024 data. A dismissed case means the debtor got no debt relief despite months or years of payments.

How long is a Chapter 13 repayment plan?

Chapter 13 plans last 3 to 5 years. If your income is below the state median, you may qualify for a 3-year plan. If above the median, your plan must be 5 years.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. One of the main benefits of Chapter 13 is the ability to keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments. The automatic stay stops foreclosure proceedings immediately upon filing.

What happens if my Chapter 13 is dismissed?

If your Chapter 13 case is dismissed, the automatic stay ends and creditors can resume collection. You may be able to file again, but there may be waiting periods.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Texas Bankruptcy Exemptions -- What You Can Keep -- Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/exemptions.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Texas Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you get to keep when you file bankruptcy. Understanding them is critical -- they can mean the difference between keeping and losing your home, car, and savings.

What Are Exemptions?

Bankruptcy exemptions are laws that protect certain property from being taken to pay your creditors. In Chapter 7, non-exempt property can be sold by the trustee. In Chapter 13, the value of non-exempt property affects how much you must pay through your plan.

Texas requires you to use state exemptions -- you cannot choose the federal exemption system.

Texas Exemptions -- Full List

Homestead -- Unlimited (10 acres urban)

Protects Unlimited (up to 10 acres urban / 100 acres rural) of equity in your primary residence. Equity is your home's current market value minus what you owe on the mortgage.

Tex. Prop. Code 41.001

Motor Vehicle -- $0 -- fully exempt if needed for work

Protects Fully exempt if needed for work (per person) of equity in your motor vehicle. If you owe more than the car is worth (you are "underwater"), there is no equity to protect and the exemption is not needed.

Tex. Prop. Code 42.002

All Texas Exemptions

PropertyTexas ExemptionStatute

HomesteadUnlimited (10 acres urban / 100 acres rural)Tex. Prop. Code 41.001

VehicleFully exempt (1 per licensed household member)Tex. Prop. Code 42.002

Personal property (aggregate)$60,000 married / $30,000 singleTex. Prop. Code 42.001

ClothingIncluded in personal propertyTex. Prop. Code 42.002

Tools of tradeUnlimitedTex. Prop. Code 42.002

Retirement accounts (IRA, 401k, pension)UnlimitedTex. Prop. Code 42.0021

Social Security benefitsUnlimited42 U.S.C. 407

Wages (current)Unlimited (non-garnishable in Texas)Tex. Prop. Code 42.001

Life insurance (cash value)UnlimitedTex. Ins. Code 1108.051

Health aidsUnlimitedTex. Prop. Code 42.002

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Texas homestead exemption?

Texas's homestead exemption protects Unlimited (up to 10 acres urban / 100 acres rural) of equity in your primary residence. Equity is the difference between your home's value and what you owe on the mortgage.

How much vehicle equity can I protect in Texas?

Texas's motor vehicle exemption is $0 -- fully exempt if needed for work. If your car equity is within the limit, you can keep it in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments through your plan.

Are retirement accounts protected?

Yes. Retirement accounts including IRAs, 401(k)s, and pensions are fully exempt in Texas. Social Security benefits are also fully exempt under federal law.

What personal property can I keep?

Texas protects $60,000 (married) / $30,000 (single) in personal property. Tools of trade up to Unlimited. Health aids and Social Security benefits are fully exempt. See the complete table above.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Houston? -- Filing Fees, Attorney Fees
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cost.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Houston?

Bankruptcy has real costs, but they are often far less than the debt you are trying to eliminate. Here is a transparent breakdown of every cost you should expect.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling (2 courses)

$1,000-$5,000

Typical Attorney Fees

Chapter 7 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$338Can pay in installments; fee waiver available

Attorney fees$1,000-$2,500Typical Houston range; usually paid upfront

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Credit report$0 - $30Free from annualcreditreport.com

Total estimate$1,400-$2,900

Fee Waiver (Chapter 7 Only)

If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you can apply to have the $338 filing fee waived entirely. For 2026, 150% of poverty for a single person is approximately $22,590. You file the fee waiver application (Official Form 103B) with your petition.

Chapter 13 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$313Can be paid through your plan

Attorney fees$3,000-$5,000Typical Houston range; mostly paid through plan

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Total estimate$3,400-$5,400Most paid through plan, not upfront

The Chapter 13 Payment Advantage

A major advantage of Chapter 13 is that attorney fees and the filing fee can be included in your repayment plan. You typically need only a small upfront payment (often $0-$500), with the rest paid through your monthly plan payments over 3-5 years.

Ways to Reduce Costs

Free and Low-Cost Options

Lone Star Legal Aid -- provides free legal services for qualifying low-income individuals

Fee waiver -- eliminates the $338 Chapter 7 filing fee for income below 150% of poverty

Installment payments -- pay the filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days

Pro se filing -- filing without an attorney is possible but not recommended (10.8% of S.D. Tex. filers do this)

Reduced-fee credit counseling -- many approved agencies offer reduced fees for low-income filers

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Houston?

The filing fee is $338. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000-$2,500. Total is typically $1,400-$2,900. Fee waivers are available for low-income filers. National cost comparison.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Houston?

The filing fee is $313 and attorney fees typically range from $3,000-$5,000. Most costs can be paid through your repayment plan.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

The filing fee can be waived in Chapter 7 if your income is below 150% of poverty. Lone Star Legal Aid may provide free representation.

Do I have to pay an attorney upfront?

For Chapter 7, most attorneys require full payment before filing. For Chapter 13, fees are typically included in your repayment plan.

## What to Expect When Filing Bankruptcy -- Timeline, 341 Meeting, Discharge -- Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/what-to-expect.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

What to Expect When Filing Bankruptcy

The bankruptcy process can feel overwhelming, but it follows a clear, predictable path. Here is exactly what happens from start to finish.

Chapter 7 Timeline

1-4 weeks before filing

Preparation

Complete a credit counseling course from an approved agency ($15-25). Gather documents: last 2 years of tax returns, 6 months of pay stubs, bank statements, vehicle titles, mortgage statements, and a list of all debts and assets.

Day 1

Filing Day

Your petition, schedules, and statements are filed with the Southern District of Texas. The automatic stay takes effect immediately. All collection calls, lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, and repossessions must stop.

Day 21-40

341 Meeting of Creditors

You attend a brief meeting of creditors. The trustee verifies your identity, asks questions about your finances, and reviews your petition. Bring photo ID and Social Security proof. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Day 21-60

Debtor Education Course

Complete a second financial education course ($10-25). This must be done before the court will grant your discharge.

Day 60-90 (after 341)

Discharge

If no objections are filed, the court enters your discharge order approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Eligible debts are eliminated.

Shortly after discharge

Case Closed

The trustee files a final report and the case is closed. Total time from filing: typically 3-4 months.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing

Preparation

Complete credit counseling. Work with your attorney to develop a feasible repayment plan.

Day 1

Filing Day

Petition and proposed plan filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures and repossessions stop immediately.

Day 30

First Plan Payment

Your first monthly payment to the Chapter 13 trustee is due 30 days after filing, even if your plan has not been confirmed yet.

Day 21-40

341 Meeting of Creditors

Similar to Chapter 7, but the trustee also reviews your proposed repayment plan and may request modifications.

Day 45-120

Confirmation Hearing

The court decides whether to approve your plan. If approved, you continue monthly payments as scheduled.

Years 1-5

Plan Payments

Make monthly payments to the trustee for 3-5 years. Report income changes. File tax returns on time.

After plan completion

Discharge

Complete the debtor education course. The court enters a section 1328 discharge, eliminating remaining eligible unsecured debts.

Important: In the S.D. Tex., 65.1% of Chapter 13 cases are dismissed before plan completion. A dismissed case means no discharge -- the debtor gets no debt relief despite months or years of payments.

The Automatic Stay -- Your Immediate Protection

The automatic stay is one of the most powerful protections in bankruptcy law. The moment your petition is filed, creditors must immediately stop:

Phone calls, letters, and emails demanding payment

Lawsuits and court proceedings against you

Wage garnishments

Bank account levies

Foreclosure proceedings

Vehicle repossession

Utility disconnections (for 20 days)

Creditors who violate the automatic stay can be held in contempt of court and ordered to pay damages.

Exception: If you have had a prior bankruptcy case dismissed within the past year, the automatic stay may be limited to 30 days or may not apply at all. Check your eligibility before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens at the 341 meeting of creditors?

The 341 meeting is a brief hearing where the trustee asks questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security proof. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay takes effect the moment your petition is filed. It stops collection calls, lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, and repossessions.

How long until I get my discharge?

In Chapter 7, about 3-4 months after filing. In Chapter 13, after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years; Chapter 13 for 7 years. Many filers see scores start recovering within 1-2 years. Learn about rebuilding credit.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Data sourced from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008--2024).

This site provides general information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

## Houston Bankruptcy FAQ -- 18 Common Questions Answered
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/faq.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Frequently Asked Questions

Answers to the 18 most common questions from Houston residents considering bankruptcy.

Cost and Eligibility

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Houston?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000-$2,500 for Chapter 7 and $3,000-$5,000 for Chapter 13. Credit counseling costs about $25-50. Full cost breakdown.

What is the means test?

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. It compares your household income to the Texas median. If below, you qualify automatically.

Can I file bankruptcy more than once?

Yes, but there are time limits. After Chapter 7, wait 8 years for another Chapter 7 or 4 years for Chapter 13. After Chapter 13, wait 6 years for Chapter 7 or 2 years for another Chapter 13. Use the 1328(f) screener to check.

Property and Assets

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy?

In Texas, your homestead exemption protects Unlimited (10 acres urban). In Chapter 13, you can keep your home and catch up on missed payments. See all exemptions.

Can I keep my car in bankruptcy?

Texas protects $0 -- fully exempt if needed for work in vehicle equity. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments. Exemption details.

What happens to my bank account after filing?

You keep your bank account, but the balance on filing date must be disclosed. If you owe money to that bank, consider switching before filing.

What happens to my tax refund in bankruptcy?

In Chapter 7, your tax refund may be considered an asset. In Chapter 13, it may need to be turned over to the trustee. Timing matters -- discuss with your attorney.

The Process

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 takes 3-4 months. Chapter 13 requires 3-5 years. The 341 meeting happens about 30 days after filing. Full timeline.

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

A brief hearing about 30 days after filing. The trustee asks questions under oath. Bring photo ID and Social Security proof. Most last 5-10 minutes. Full guide.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay takes effect immediately when you file. It stops lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, repossessions, and collection calls.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay stops garnishments immediately. However, garnishments for child support and certain tax debts may continue. Learn more.

Types of Debt

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical bills are fully dischargeable in both chapters. Medical debt is one of the most common reasons people file.

Will bankruptcy eliminate my student loans?

Generally no. Student loans require proving undue hardship in a separate proceeding. Learn more.

Can creditors still call me after I file?

No. The automatic stay prohibits all collection activity. Report violations to your attorney.

Life After Bankruptcy

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Chapter 7 stays on your report for 10 years; Chapter 13 for 7 years. Many filers see recovery within 1-2 years. Rebuilding after bankruptcy.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 is faster but requires a means test. Chapter 13 lets you keep property and catch up on payments. Compare them.

Can I file bankruptcy if I am married?

Yes. File individually or jointly. If individually, your spouse's separate property and credit are generally not affected.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

Ch. 7 vs 13

## Houston Bankruptcy Statistics -- 91,806 Cases Analyzed
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/statistics.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Houston Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from 91,806 Chapter 13 cases in the Southern District of Texas, analyzed from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008-2024).

91,806

Total Ch. 13 Cases (2008-2024)

65.1%

Dismissal Rate

34.9%

Discharge Rate

30.6%

Prior Filer Rate

2,622

Est. 1328(f) Violations

10.8%

Pro Se Rate

What These Numbers Mean for You

If you are considering Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Houston, these statistics should inform your decision:

65.1% of Chapter 13 cases in the S.D. Tex. are dismissed. That means many filers spent months or years making payments to a trustee and got no debt relief at the end.

30.6% of filers had a previous bankruptcy case. This "prior filer" rate suggests many people are cycling through the system without achieving lasting relief.

An estimated 2,622 cases may involve 1328(f) eligibility issues. These are cases where the debtor may have been ineligible for a discharge from the start.

Only 10.8% of filers represent themselves. Quality legal representation is critical to completing a Chapter 13 plan.

S.D. Tex. Overview

MetricS.D. Tex.

Total Ch. 13 cases (2008-2024)91,806

Dismissal rate65.1%

Discharge rate34.9%

Prior filer rate30.6%

Pro se rate10.8%

Est. 1328(f) violations2,622

Key Takeaways

The Prior Filer Problem

30.6% of S.D. Tex. filers had a prior bankruptcy case. This cycling through the system suggests the first filing failed to provide lasting relief -- and raises questions about whether filers were properly screened under section 1328(f).

2,622 Potential 1328(f) Violations

An estimated 2,622 cases in the S.D. Tex. may involve potential eligibility violations under 11 U.S.C. section 1328(f) -- filings where the debtor had a prior discharge within the statutory time bar and could never have received a new discharge. These cases consume court resources and cost consumers time and money for a legally impossible outcome.

Representation Matters

With only 10.8% of filers representing themselves, the data confirms that legal representation is the norm in the S.D. Tex.. Given the 65.1% dismissal rate, the quality of that representation is critical to whether a Chapter 13 plan succeeds or fails.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many bankruptcy cases are filed in Houston?

Between 2008 and 2024, the S.D. Tex. saw 91,806 Chapter 13 filings. Volumes peaked around 2010-2011 following the financial crisis.

What is the Chapter 13 success rate in the S.D. Tex.?

34.9% of cases achieved a discharge. 65.1% were dismissed. The prior filer rate is 30.6%.

What are 1328(f) violations?

Cases where the debtor had a prior discharge within the statutory time bar under section 1328(f) and could never have received a new discharge. An estimated 2,622 such cases exist in the S.D. Tex.. Methodology details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Houston Bankruptcy Resources -- Courts, Legal Aid, Credit Counseling
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/resources.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Houston Bankruptcy Resources

Courts, legal aid organizations, credit counseling agencies, and free educational tools to help you navigate the bankruptcy process.

Bankruptcy Court

Southern District of Texas -- Bankruptcy Court

Address: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

Phone: (713) 250-5500

Website: txs.uscourts.gov

PACER: ecf.txsb.uscourts.gov

Legal Aid Organizations

Lone Star Legal Aid

Provides free civil legal services to low-income individuals, including bankruptcy representation.

Phone: (800) 733-8394

Website: lonestarlegal.org

Houston Volunteer Lawyers

Phone: (713) 228-0735

Website: makejusticehappen.org

Houston Bar Association -- Lawyer Referral Service

Can connect you with attorneys who handle bankruptcy cases.

Phone: (713) 237-9429

Website: hba.org

Credit Counseling and Debtor Education

Two courses are required for bankruptcy: a pre-filing credit counseling course and a post-filing debtor education course. You must use agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee Program.

Approved agencies: justice.gov -- approved agencies

Cost: $15-25 per course (reduced fees available for low-income filers)

Format: Online, phone, or in-person

Time: About 60-90 minutes per course

U.S. Trustee Program

The U.S. Trustee Program oversees the administration of bankruptcy cases.

Region 7 (includes S.D. Tex.): (713) 718-4650

Website: justice.gov/ust

Free Educational Tools

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

1328(f) Discharge Eligibility Screener -- check if a prior discharge affects your eligibility

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 Comparison

Automatic Stay Guide

341 Meeting Guide

Means Test Explained

National Cost Guide

How to File Bankruptcy

Case Dismissed?

File Again?

Non-Dischargeable Debts

Dismissal Rate Data

Frequently Asked Questions

Where is the Houston bankruptcy court?

Southern District of Texas: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002 ((713) 250-5500).

Is there free legal help for bankruptcy in Houston?

Yes. Lone Star Legal Aid ((800) 733-8394) and Houston Volunteer Lawyers ((713) 228-0735) provide free legal services for qualifying individuals.

Where can I take the required credit counseling course?

The U.S. Trustee Program maintains a list of approved agencies at justice.gov/ust. Most offer online courses for $15-25.

## Bankruptcy by County -- Houston Metro Area
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/counties.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Bankruptcy by County -- Houston Metro Area

Your county determines which federal court handles your bankruptcy case and which state exemptions protect your property. Find your county below for specific information.

5 Counties in the Houston Metro

Harris County

Harris County is the most populous county in Texas and the core of the Houston metro area. As the county seat of Houston, it handles the largest volume of bankruptcy filings in the Southern District of Texas.

District: Southern District of Texas (Houston Division)

Courthouse: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

Harris County bankruptcy guide →

Fort Bend County

Fort Bend County is one of the fastest-growing counties in Texas, located southwest of Houston. Residents file bankruptcy in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division.

District: Southern District of Texas (Houston Division)

Courthouse: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

Fort Bend County bankruptcy guide →

Montgomery County

Montgomery County is located north of Houston and includes The Woodlands and Conroe. Bankruptcy cases are filed in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division.

District: Southern District of Texas (Houston Division)

Courthouse: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

Montgomery County bankruptcy guide →

Brazoria County

Brazoria County is located south of Houston along the Gulf Coast. Unlike the other Houston metro counties, Brazoria County falls under the Galveston Division of the Southern District of Texas.

District: Southern District of Texas (Galveston Division)

Courthouse: 601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550

Brazoria County bankruptcy guide →

Galveston County

Galveston County sits on the Gulf Coast southeast of Houston. It is part of the Galveston Division of the Southern District of Texas and has its own federal courthouse.

District: Southern District of Texas (Galveston Division)

Courthouse: 601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550

Galveston County bankruptcy guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which counties are in the Houston bankruptcy metro area?

The Houston metro area covers 5 counties: Harris County, Fort Bend County, Montgomery County, Brazoria County, Galveston County. Each county falls under a specific federal bankruptcy court district.

Does it matter which county I live in for bankruptcy?

Yes. Your county determines which federal district court handles your case, and potentially which state exemptions apply. Different districts may have different local rules, trustees, and dismissal rates.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Harris County -- Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/counties/harris-county.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Harris County

Bankruptcy in Harris County

Harris County is the most populous county in Texas and the core of the Houston metro area. As the county seat of Houston, it handles the largest volume of bankruptcy filings in the Southern District of Texas.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of Texas (Houston Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Harris County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Texas Exemptions in Harris County

Texas offers some of the most generous bankruptcy exemptions in the country. The homestead exemption is unlimited in acreage for urban properties up to 10 acres (or 100 acres for rural). Personal property exemptions include up to $50,000 for a single filer or $100,000 for a family in aggregate personal property. Vehicle exemption: one motor vehicle per licensed household member. Texas does not allow federal exemptions -- you must use the state exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Harris County

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- The petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas (Houston Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Harris County cases?

Harris County bankruptcy cases are filed in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The courthouse is located at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002. Both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 cases for Harris County residents are handled at this location.

What exemptions apply in Harris County?

Texas state exemptions apply. The homestead exemption is unlimited in value for urban properties up to 10 acres. Personal property exemptions total up to $50,000 (single) or $100,000 (family). Each licensed driver can exempt one motor vehicle.

How many bankruptcy cases are filed in Harris County?

Harris County consistently has the highest volume of bankruptcy filings in the Houston metro area, reflecting its large population of over 4.7 million residents. The Southern District of Texas -- Houston Division handles thousands of cases annually.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Other Houston Area Counties

Fort Bend County
Montgomery County
Brazoria County
Galveston County
All Counties →

## Bankruptcy in Fort Bend County -- Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/counties/fort-bend-county.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Fort Bend County

Bankruptcy in Fort Bend County

Fort Bend County is one of the fastest-growing counties in Texas, located southwest of Houston. Residents file bankruptcy in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of Texas (Houston Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Fort Bend County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Texas Exemptions in Fort Bend County

Texas state exemptions apply in Fort Bend County. The homestead exemption is unlimited in value for urban properties up to 10 acres (100 acres rural). Personal property exemptions total up to $50,000 (single) or $100,000 (family). Each licensed household member can exempt one motor vehicle. Texas does not allow the use of federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Fort Bend County

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- The petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas (Houston Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Fort Bend County cases?

Fort Bend County cases are filed in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division, at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002. Fort Bend County residents travel to the Houston courthouse for their 341 meeting of creditors and any court hearings.

What is the homestead exemption in Fort Bend County?

Texas has one of the most generous homestead exemptions in the nation -- unlimited in value. For urban properties (which includes most of Fort Bend County), the acreage limit is 10 acres. This means most Fort Bend homeowners can protect their home equity fully in bankruptcy.

Is Fort Bend County considered urban or rural for exemptions?

Most of Fort Bend County is classified as urban for Texas exemption purposes, which means the 10-acre homestead limit applies. Some outlying areas may qualify as rural (100-acre limit). The classification depends on the specific location of your property.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Other Houston Area Counties

Harris County
Montgomery County
Brazoria County
Galveston County

## Bankruptcy in Montgomery County -- Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/counties/montgomery-county.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Montgomery County

Bankruptcy in Montgomery County

Montgomery County is located north of Houston and includes The Woodlands and Conroe. Bankruptcy cases are filed in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of Texas (Houston Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Montgomery County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Texas Exemptions in Montgomery County

Texas state exemptions apply. Homestead exemption: unlimited value, up to 10 acres urban or 100 acres rural. Personal property: up to $50,000 (single) or $100,000 (family). One motor vehicle per licensed household member. Texas does not permit federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Montgomery County

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- The petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas (Houston Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Montgomery County residents file bankruptcy?

Montgomery County residents file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The courthouse is at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002. Your 341 meeting of creditors will typically be held in Houston.

Can I keep my home in Montgomery County if I file bankruptcy?

Texas offers an unlimited homestead exemption in value. If your Montgomery County property is 10 acres or less (urban) or 100 acres or less (rural), your home equity is fully protected. Many Montgomery County properties may qualify for the rural 100-acre exemption depending on location.

What is the median income for the means test in Montgomery County?

The means test uses Texas state median income figures, not county-specific numbers. For a household of one, the Texas median is approximately $59,000. Households above the median must pass additional calculations to qualify for Chapter 7.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Fort Bend County
Brazoria County

## Bankruptcy in Brazoria County -- Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/counties/brazoria-county.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Brazoria County

Bankruptcy in Brazoria County

Brazoria County is located south of Houston along the Gulf Coast. Unlike the other Houston metro counties, Brazoria County falls under the Galveston Division of the Southern District of Texas.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of Texas (Galveston Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Brazoria County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Texas Exemptions in Brazoria County

Texas state exemptions apply in Brazoria County. Homestead: unlimited value, 10 acres urban / 100 acres rural. Personal property: up to $50,000 (single) or $100,000 (family). One vehicle per licensed driver. Federal exemptions are not available in Texas.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Brazoria County

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- The petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas (Galveston Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which court division handles Brazoria County bankruptcies?

Brazoria County cases are filed in the Southern District of Texas, Galveston Division. The courthouse is at 601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550. This is different from other Houston metro counties which use the Houston Division.

Do the same exemptions apply in Brazoria County as in Houston?

Yes. Texas state exemptions apply uniformly across all counties. The homestead exemption is unlimited in value (10 acres urban, 100 acres rural). Personal property exemptions total up to $50,000 (single) or $100,000 (family).

How far is the Galveston courthouse from Brazoria County?

The Galveston Division courthouse is approximately 30-50 miles from most Brazoria County cities. Pearland residents are about 45 minutes away; Angleton residents are about 40 minutes away. Some hearings may be conducted by phone or video.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Harris County
Fort Bend County
Montgomery County
Galveston County

## Bankruptcy in Galveston County -- Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/counties/galveston-county.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Galveston County

Bankruptcy in Galveston County

Galveston County sits on the Gulf Coast southeast of Houston. It is part of the Galveston Division of the Southern District of Texas and has its own federal courthouse.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of Texas (Galveston Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Galveston County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Texas Exemptions in Galveston County

Texas state exemptions apply. Homestead: unlimited in value, 10 acres urban / 100 acres rural. Personal property: $50,000 (single) / $100,000 (family). One motor vehicle per licensed household member. Federal exemptions not available in Texas.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Galveston County

The Process

Credit counseling -- Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition -- The petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas (Galveston Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550.

Debtor education -- Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge -- Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where is the bankruptcy courthouse for Galveston County?

Galveston County residents file in the Southern District of Texas, Galveston Division. The courthouse is conveniently located at 601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550, within the county itself.

What are the filing fees for bankruptcy in Galveston County?

Filing fees are the same nationwide: $338 for Chapter 7 and $313 for Chapter 13. Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Chapter 13 filing fees can be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I protect my boat in Galveston County bankruptcy?

Texas exemptions do not specifically exempt boats. However, the generous personal property exemption ($50,000 single / $100,000 family) may cover a boat if you have room within your aggregate exemption. Consult a qualified attorney for asset-specific advice.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Find Your Bankruptcy District -- Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/district-lookup.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Find Your Bankruptcy District

Enter your zip code to find which federal bankruptcy court handles cases in your area of the Houston metro.

Zip Code Lookup

Look Up

Texas Bankruptcy Districts

Southern District of Texas (S.D. Tex.)

Covers the Houston metropolitan area including Harris, Fort Bend, Montgomery, Galveston, Brazoria, and surrounding counties. Most zip codes starting with 770-775 and 77xxx fall here.

Courthouse: 515 Rusk St, Houston, TX 77002

Divisional offices: Houston, Galveston, Corpus Christi, Laredo, McAllen, Brownsville, Victoria

Northern District of Texas (N.D. Tex.)

Covers the Dallas-Fort Worth area and north Texas. Zip codes typically starting with 75xxx and 76xxx.

Courthouse: 1100 Commerce St, Dallas, TX 75242

Eastern District of Texas (E.D. Tex.)

Covers east Texas including Tyler, Beaumont, and Texarkana. Some 75xxx zip codes in eastern portions of the state.

Courthouse: 110 N College Ave, Tyler, TX 75702

Western District of Texas (W.D. Tex.)

Covers San Antonio, Austin, El Paso, Waco, and central/west Texas. Zip codes typically starting with 73xxx, 78xxx, and 79xxx.

Courthouse: 615 E Houston St, San Antonio, TX 78205

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy district covers Houston?

Houston is in the Southern District of Texas. The courthouse is located at 515 Rusk St, Houston, TX 77002. Most Harris County and surrounding county zip codes (770xx-775xx) fall within this district.

How do I know which Texas bankruptcy district I'm in?

Texas has four federal bankruptcy districts: Southern (Houston area), Northern (Dallas/Fort Worth), Eastern (Tyler/Beaumont), and Western (San Antonio/Austin). Your zip code determines your district. Use the lookup tool above to find yours.

Can I file bankruptcy in a different district than where I live?

Generally no. You must file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days, where your principal assets are located, or where your domicile is. Filing in the wrong district can result in your case being dismissed or transferred.

## Free Legal Help for Bankruptcy in Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/legal-aid.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Free Legal Help for Bankruptcy in Houston

If you are struggling with debt but cannot afford a bankruptcy attorney, these Houston-area organizations may be able to help -- for free or at reduced cost.

Houston Legal Aid Organizations

Lone Star Legal Aid

Phone: (713) 652-0077

Website: lonestarlegal.org

Lone Star Legal Aid provides free civil legal assistance to low-income residents across the Houston area and Southeast Texas. They handle a range of consumer and debt-related matters, including bankruptcy guidance and representation for qualifying individuals.

Houston Volunteer Lawyers

Phone: (713) 228-0732

Website: makejusticehappen.org

Houston Volunteer Lawyers connects low-income Houstonians with volunteer attorneys who provide free legal help. Their programs cover a variety of civil legal matters, and they may be able to match you with an attorney experienced in bankruptcy filings.

South Texas College of Law Houston -- Pro Bono Program

South Texas College of Law Houston operates pro bono legal clinics where law students, supervised by licensed attorneys, provide free legal assistance. These clinics periodically handle consumer debt and bankruptcy matters. Contact the school's clinical programs office for current availability and scheduling.

Who Qualifies for Legal Aid?

Most legal aid organizations serve people whose household income is at or below 125-200% of the federal poverty level. For reference, the 2026 guidelines are approximately:

Single person: $15,060 (100% FPL) -- up to ~$30,120 (200% FPL)

Family of 2: $20,440 -- up to ~$40,880

Family of 4: $31,200 -- up to ~$62,400

Each organization sets its own income limits. Even if you are slightly above the threshold, it is worth calling -- some programs make exceptions based on circumstances such as medical emergencies, domestic violence, or sudden job loss.

Filing on Your Own (Pro Se)

If you do not qualify for legal aid but cannot afford an attorney, you have the right to file bankruptcy on your own. This is called filing "pro se." It is more common in Chapter 7 cases, which are generally simpler than Chapter 13.

The Pro Se Bankruptcy Guide provides step-by-step instructions for people filing without an attorney.

You can also reduce costs by:

Requesting a filing fee waiver (Chapter 7 only, if income is below 150% FPL)

Paying the filing fee in installments

Using free credit counseling agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee

Pro Se Filing Guide

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I get free bankruptcy help in Houston?

Yes. Several organizations in Houston provide free legal assistance for bankruptcy cases, including Lone Star Legal Aid and Houston Volunteer Lawyers. Eligibility is generally based on income, typically at or below 125-200% of the federal poverty level.

Do I qualify for legal aid?

Most legal aid organizations in Houston serve people whose income is at or below 125-200% of the federal poverty level. For a single person in 2026, that is roughly $18,000-$30,000 per year. Family size affects the threshold. Contact the organization directly for specific eligibility requirements.

What if I make too much for legal aid?

If your income is too high for legal aid, consider filing pro se (on your own) using resources at prosebankruptcy.org. Some attorneys offer payment plans or flat-fee arrangements. You can also check if any local law school clinics have openings.

What documents do I need to apply for free bankruptcy help?

Most legal aid organizations will ask for proof of income (pay stubs, tax returns, benefits letters), identification, and a summary of your debts and assets. Bring as much documentation as you can to your first appointment to speed up the process.

## Houston Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7?
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/means-test.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Houston Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7?

The means test determines whether you can file Chapter 7 bankruptcy. Your income is compared to the Texas median income for your household size. Here is how it works for Houston residents filing in the S.D. Tex..

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is the Means Test?

The means test was introduced by the Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention and Consumer Protection Act of 2005 (BAPCPA). Its purpose is to determine whether a debtor's Chapter 7 filing constitutes an "abuse" of the bankruptcy system based on income.

The test works in two parts:

Part 1 -- Income comparison: Your average monthly income over the past six months is compared to the median income for your household size in Texas. If you are below median, you qualify for Chapter 7 automatically.

Part 2 -- Disposable income calculation: If you are above median, the test subtracts allowed expenses from your income. If your remaining disposable income is too low to fund a meaningful Chapter 13 plan, you still qualify for Chapter 7.

2026 Texas Median Income Thresholds

If you live in Houston, these are the Texas income thresholds for the means test. Your total household income over the past six months (annualized) must be below these figures to pass Part 1.

Household SizeAnnual Median IncomeMonthly Equivalent

1 person$60,840$5,070

2 persons$78,458$6,538

3 persons$85,196$7,100

4 persons$103,898$8,658

Each additional personAdd approximately $9,900

Houston filers are in the S.D. Tex.. Courthouse: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002.

Step-by-Step Means Test Walkthrough

1Calculate your current monthly income (CMI)

Add up all income from all sources for the six full calendar months before your filing date. Divide by six to get your average monthly income. Multiply by 12 to get your annualized figure. Sources include wages, salary, tips, bonuses, overtime, self-employment income, rental income, pension, disability (non-Social Security), alimony received, and any other regular income.

2Determine your household size

Count everyone who lives in your household and depends on your income for support. This typically includes yourself, your spouse (even if not filing jointly), and dependent children.

3Compare to Texas median income

Compare your annualized CMI to the Texas median income for your household size (see table above). If your income is at or below the median, you pass the means test and qualify for Chapter 7.

4If above median: Calculate allowable deductions

If your income exceeds the median, you proceed to Part 2. You deduct IRS-approved living expenses, actual secured debt payments (mortgage, car loans), priority debts, and certain other allowed expenses. These deductions are partly standardized (IRS Local Standards for the Houston area) and partly based on your actual expenses.

5Calculate disposable income

Subtract your total allowed deductions from your CMI. Multiply the remaining monthly disposable income by 60 (representing a 5-year Chapter 13 plan). If the result is less than $9,075, you pass. If more than $15,150, you fail. Between those amounts, further analysis is needed.

6File the appropriate form

The means test is filed on Official Form 122A-1 and, if needed, Form 122A-2. Your attorney will prepare these as part of your petition filed in the S.D. Tex..

What Happens If You Fail the Means Test?

Failing the means test does not mean you cannot file bankruptcy. You have several options:

File Chapter 13 instead: Chapter 13 has no means test. You repay debts over 3-5 years while keeping your property.

Challenge with special circumstances: Document medical conditions, military service, or other factors justifying additional expenses.

Wait and refile: Since the test uses the six months before filing, you may qualify later if income decreases.

Re-examine deductions: An experienced attorney may identify overlooked deductions -- health insurance, mandatory retirement contributions, childcare costs, or education expenses.

File Chapter 11 Subchapter V: For small business owners, Subchapter V may be an option without a traditional means test.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the bankruptcy means test?

The means test is a calculation required for Chapter 7 bankruptcy that compares your income to the median income in Texas. If your income is below the median for your household size, you automatically qualify for Chapter 7. If above, you must pass a second calculation that factors in your allowed expenses.

What is the median income for the means test in Texas in 2026?

The 2026 Texas median income thresholds are: 1-person household $60,840, 2-person $78,458, 3-person $85,196, 4-person $103,898. Add approximately $9,900 for each additional household member beyond four.

What happens if I fail the means test in Houston?

If you fail the means test, you can file Chapter 13 instead (no means test required), wait until your income drops below the median, challenge the presumption of abuse with special circumstances, or work with an attorney to re-examine your allowable deductions.

Does the means test apply to Chapter 13?

No, the means test does not apply to Chapter 13 bankruptcy. However, your income level determines whether your Chapter 13 plan lasts 3 years (below median) or 5 years (above median), and how much you must pay to unsecured creditors.

What income counts for the means test?

The means test uses your average monthly income from all sources over the six full calendar months before filing. This includes wages, salary, tips, bonuses, overtime, business income, rental income, pension, unemployment, and other regular income. Social Security benefits are excluded from the initial median income comparison.

Where do I file the means test in Houston?

The means test is filed with your bankruptcy petition in the S.D. Tex.. The courthouse is located at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002. The means test form is Official Form 122A-1 (and 122A-2 if above median).

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## The Automatic Stay in Houston Bankruptcy
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/automatic-stay.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

The Automatic Stay in Houston Bankruptcy

The moment you file bankruptcy in the S.D. Tex., a powerful federal court order called the automatic stay takes effect. Here is what it means for Houston residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is the Automatic Stay?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 362, the automatic stay is an injunction that immediately halts nearly all collection efforts against you the instant your bankruptcy petition is filed with the S.D. Tex.. It applies to all creditors, whether they know about the filing or not.

For Houston residents, this means that foreclosure proceedings, wage garnishment, vehicle repossession, debt collection lawsuits, harassing phone calls, bank levies, and utility shutoffs must stop immediately.

What the Automatic Stay Stops

Foreclosure

Non-Judicial sales halted

Garnishment

Wage deductions stopped

Repossession

Vehicle seizure blocked

Lawsuits

Collection suits frozen

Foreclosure: Texas uses non-judicial foreclosure. The automatic stay halts the process regardless of how far along it is.

Wage garnishment: Wages cannot be garnished for consumer debt in TX. The automatic stay stops all garnishment that is in progress.

Vehicle repossession: Creditors cannot repossess your car after you file. If your car was recently repossessed, you may be able to recover it.

Collection lawsuits: All pending lawsuits for debt collection are stayed.

Phone calls and letters: Debt collectors must stop all contact immediately.

Bank levies: Creditors cannot freeze or seize your bank accounts.

Utility shutoffs: Utilities cannot disconnect service for 20 days after filing.

What the Automatic Stay Does NOT Stop

Child support and alimony: Domestic support obligations continue unaffected.

Criminal proceedings: The stay does not halt criminal cases.

Certain tax proceedings: Tax audits and tax court proceedings may continue.

Prior eviction judgments: If the landlord already obtained a judgment for possession, the eviction may proceed.

Pension loan repayment: Payroll deductions for retirement plan loans continue.

Stay Violations in the S.D. Tex.

If a creditor continues collection activity after you file bankruptcy, they are violating the automatic stay. Under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k), you may be entitled to:

Actual damages -- any financial harm caused by the violation

Attorney fees and costs -- the cost of enforcing the stay

Punitive damages -- in cases of willful violation

Document every violation carefully: save voicemails, letters, garnishment notices, and any evidence of continued collection.

Repeat Filers: Limited Stay Protection

One dismissal in the past year: The stay lasts only 30 days unless you file a motion to extend it and show good faith (section 362(c)(3)).

Two or more dismissals in the past year: The stay does not go into effect at all unless you file a motion to impose it (section 362(c)(4)).

If you are a repeat filer, timing your petition and filing the appropriate motions immediately is critical.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the automatic stay in bankruptcy?

The automatic stay is a federal court order under 11 U.S.C. section 362 that takes effect the instant you file bankruptcy in the S.D. Tex.. It immediately stops most collection actions including wage garnishment, foreclosure proceedings, repossession attempts, lawsuits, harassing phone calls, and utility shutoffs.

What does the automatic stay stop?

The automatic stay stops: wage garnishment, foreclosure sales, vehicle repossession, debt collection lawsuits, creditor phone calls and letters, bank account levies, utility disconnection (for 20 days), eviction (in some cases), and IRS collection activity.

What does the automatic stay NOT stop?

The automatic stay does not stop: child support and alimony collection (domestic support obligations), most criminal proceedings, certain tax audit proceedings, and evictions where the landlord already had a judgment for possession before filing.

What happens if a creditor violates the automatic stay in Houston?

If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you may be entitled to actual damages, attorney fees, and in some cases punitive damages under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k). Document the violation and notify your attorney immediately. The S.D. Tex. takes stay violations seriously.

Does the automatic stay apply if I filed bankruptcy before?

If you had a bankruptcy case dismissed within the past year, the automatic stay in your new case lasts only 30 days unless the court extends it. Two or more dismissals in the past year means no stay at all unless the court orders it under section 362(c)(3) and (c)(4).

How long does the automatic stay last?

In Chapter 7, the stay lasts until your case is closed, dismissed, or discharge is granted (typically 3-4 months). In Chapter 13, it lasts for the duration of your 3-5 year plan. Creditors can file a motion for relief from stay.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/credit-after-bankruptcy.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Houston

Bankruptcy is not the end of your financial life -- it is a fresh start. Here is a practical timeline and action plan for Houston residents to rebuild credit after bankruptcy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Your Credit Score After Bankruptcy

Most people see a significant drop in their credit score when bankruptcy is filed, but the actual impact depends on where your score was before filing. Many filers report scores in the 500-550 range immediately after discharge.

The good news: your score will start climbing immediately if you take the right steps. Most Houston filers can reach the 650-700 range within 18-24 months of discharge.

10 yrs

Ch. 7 on credit report

7 yrs

Ch. 13 on credit report

18 mo

Typical time to 650+ score

2 yrs

FHA mortgage eligible

Step-by-Step Credit Rebuilding Plan

1Get a secured credit card (Month 1)

Deposit $200-500 which becomes your credit limit. Use for small purchases and pay the full balance monthly. Texas Dow Employees Credit Union (TDECU), JSC Federal Credit Union, Houston Federal Credit Union all offer secured cards.

2Become an authorized user (Month 1-3)

Ask a family member with good credit to add you as an authorized user. Their positive payment history will appear on your credit report.

3Get a credit-builder loan (Month 3-6)

The bank holds the loan amount in savings while you make payments. After payoff, you get the money. This adds an installment loan to your credit mix.

4Monitor your credit reports (Ongoing)

Check all three bureaus regularly. Dispute any debts that should show as discharged but still appear active.

5Apply for an unsecured card (Month 12-18)

After 12-18 months of on-time payments, you should qualify for an unsecured card. Some issuers auto-graduate secured cards.

Major Purchase Timeline After Bankruptcy

PurchaseAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

Credit cardImmediatelyImmediately

Car loanImmediatelyImmediately (with court approval)

FHA mortgage2 years after discharge1 year into plan (with approval)

Conventional mortgage4 years after discharge2 years after discharge

VA mortgage2 years after discharge1 year after discharge

USDA mortgage3 years after discharge1 year after discharge

Local Resources for Houston Residents

Credit Unions

Texas Dow Employees Credit Union (TDECU), JSC Federal Credit Union, Houston Federal Credit Union are excellent options for post-bankruptcy banking and credit rebuilding. Credit unions are member-owned and often more willing to work with people rebuilding after bankruptcy.

Legal Aid

If you need help with credit report disputes or post-bankruptcy issues: Lone Star Legal Aid (1-800-733-8394), Houston Volunteer Lawyers (713-228-0732).

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit report in Texas?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, the impact on your score diminishes significantly after 2-3 years with active rebuilding.

Can I get a credit card after bankruptcy in Houston?

Yes. Many people receive offers within weeks of discharge. A secured credit card from Texas Dow Employees Credit Union (TDECU) is the best way to start rebuilding.

When can I buy a house after bankruptcy?

FHA loans are available 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge or 1 year into a Chapter 13 plan. Conventional loans require a 4-year wait after Chapter 7 or 2 years after Chapter 13 discharge. VA loans require 2 years after Chapter 7.

When can I buy a car after bankruptcy?

You can buy a car immediately after filing. Interest rates start higher (10-20%) but drop significantly as you rebuild credit. Many lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy auto financing.

How fast can I rebuild my credit score?

Most people reach 650-700 within 18-24 months of discharge by using a secured credit card responsibly, making all payments on time, and keeping utilization below 30%.

Does Texas have any special credit protection laws?

Texas's debtor-friendly exemption laws mean many filers emerge from bankruptcy with significant assets intact, giving them a stronger foundation for rebuilding credit. Houston has numerous credit unions like TDECU and JSC FCU that offer secured credit cards and credit-builder loans to post-bankruptcy filers.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Can I Keep My Car in Houston Bankruptcy?
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/keep-your-car.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Can I Keep My Car in Houston Bankruptcy?

For most Houston residents, the answer is yes. Texas provides a vehicle exemption of Fully exempt if needed for work, and Chapter 13 offers additional options.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Texas Vehicle Exemption

Texas's vehicle exemption protects fully exempt (any vehicle needed for occupation under Tex. Prop. Code section 42.002). Vehicle equity is calculated as the fair market value minus what you owe on the loan.

Example: If your car is worth $12,000 and you owe $10,000, your equity is $2,000. This equity is fully protected under Texas law since the vehicle is needed for work.

Options for Keeping Your Car

Chapter 7 Options

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The debt survives discharge.

Redemption: Pay the car's current value in one lump sum, even if you owe more (section 722).

Ride-through: Continue making payments without reaffirming (not available in all circuits).

Chapter 13 Options

Keep and pay: Include car payments in your 3-5 year plan.

Cramdown: If the car was purchased 910+ days before filing, pay only current value.

Cure arrears: Catch up on missed payments through the plan.

Chapter 13 Cramdown Explained

Under 11 U.S.C. section 1325(a), if you purchased your vehicle more than 910 days (approximately 2.5 years) before filing:

You pay the vehicle's current fair market value, not the loan balance

The interest rate is set by the court (typically prime + 1-3%)

The remaining loan balance is treated as unsecured debt

Example: You owe $18,000 on a car worth $10,000. Through cramdown, you pay approximately $10,000 plus interest. The $8,000 difference is treated as unsecured debt.

What If Your Car Was Already Repossessed?

If your car was recently repossessed in Houston, time is critical. Filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which can force the creditor to return the vehicle if it has not been sold yet.

Contact a Houston bankruptcy attorney immediately if your car has been repossessed or is about to be.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy in Houston?

In most cases, yes. Texas allows a vehicle exemption of Fully exempt if needed for work. If you owe more than the car is worth, your equity is protected. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car regardless of equity.

What is the vehicle exemption in Texas?

Texas allows a vehicle exemption of fully exempt (any vehicle needed for occupation under Tex. Prop. Code section 42.002).

What is a reaffirmation agreement?

A reaffirmation agreement is a new contract with your car lender that keeps you personally liable for the debt after bankruptcy. You continue making payments and keep the car. The debt survives your discharge.

What is redemption in bankruptcy?

Redemption under 11 U.S.C. section 722 allows you to keep your car by paying the lender the current fair market value in a single lump-sum payment, even if you owe more.

What is a Chapter 13 cramdown on a car loan?

In Chapter 13, if you purchased your car more than 910 days before filing, you can cram down the loan to the car's current value. You pay only the vehicle's value through your plan at a court-approved interest rate.

What happens if my car is repossessed before I file in Houston?

If your car was recently repossessed, filing bankruptcy may allow you to recover it. The automatic stay can force the creditor to return the vehicle if it has not been sold yet.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

1328(f) Screener
